**Monoamine oxidases** (**MAO**) ([EC](/wiki/Enzyme_Commission_number "Enzyme Commission number") [1.4.3.4](https://enzyme.expasy.org/EC/1.4.3.4)) are a family of [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") that [catalyze](/wiki/Catalysis "Catalysis") the [oxidation](/wiki/Oxidation "Oxidation") of [monoamines](/wiki/Monoamine "Monoamine"), employing oxygen to clip off their amine group.[[1]](#cite_note-pmid15279561-1)[[2]](#cite_note-pmid15279562-2) They are found bound to the outer membrane of [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria") in most cell types of the body. The first such enzyme was discovered in 1928 by [Mary Bernheim](/wiki/Mary_Bernheim "Mary Bernheim") in the liver and was named tyramine oxidase.[[3]](#cite_note-pmid16744124-3)[[4]](#cite_note-pmid10643441-4) The MAOs belong to the [protein family](/wiki/Protein_family "Protein family") of [flavin-containing amine oxidoreductases](/wiki/Flavin-containing_amine_oxidoreductase "Flavin-containing amine oxidoreductase").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

MAOs are important in the breakdown of monoamines ingested in food, and also serve to inactivate [monoamine neurotransmitters](/wiki/Monoamine_neurotransmitter "Monoamine neurotransmitter"). Because of the latter, they are involved in a number of psychiatric and neurological diseases, some of which can be treated with [monoamine oxidase inhibitors](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitors "Monoamine oxidase inhibitors") (MAOIs) which block the action of MAOs.[[6]](#cite_note-pmid31191248-6)

## Subtypes and tissue distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Subtypes and tissue distribution")]

In humans there are two types of MAO: [MAO-A](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_A "Monoamine oxidase A") and [MAO-B](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_B "Monoamine oxidase B").[[7]](#cite_note-pmid15279563-7)

* Both are found in [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") and [astroglia](/wiki/Astroglia "Astroglia").
* Outside the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system"):
  + MAO-A is also found in the [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver"), [pulmonary vascular](/wiki/Pulmonary_vascular_system "Pulmonary vascular system") [endothelium](/wiki/Endothelium "Endothelium"), [gastrointestinal tract](/wiki/Human_gastrointestinal_tract "Human gastrointestinal tract"), and [placenta](/wiki/Placenta "Placenta").
  + MAO-B is mostly found in [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood") [platelets](/wiki/Platelet "Platelet").

MAO-A appears at roughly 80% of adulthood levels at birth, increasing very slightly after the first 4 years of life, while MAO-B is almost non-detectable in the infant brain. Regional distribution of the monoamine oxidases is characterized by extremely high levels of both MAOs in the [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus") and hippocampal uncus, as well as a large amount of MAO-B with very little MAO-A in the [striatum](/wiki/Striatum "Striatum") and [globus pallidus](/wiki/Globus_pallidus "Globus pallidus"). The cortex has relatively high levels of only MAO-A, with the exception of areas of the [cingulate cortex](/wiki/Cingulate_cortex "Cingulate cortex"), which contains a balance of both. Autopsied brains demonstrated the predicted increased concentration of MAO-A in regions dense in serotonergic neurotransmission, however MAO-B only correlated with norepinephrine.[[8]](#cite_note-pmid23403377-8)

Other studies, in which the activities of MAO (not protein amounts) were examined in rat brain, revealed the highest MAO-B activity in the median eminence of hypothalamus. Dorsal raphe nucleus and medial preoptic area have relatively high MAO-B activity, but much lower than MAO-B activity in the median eminence.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10) Among cerebral endocrine glands, pineal gland has high MAO-B activity (its median value is lower than that for median eminence and higher than that for medial preoptic area).[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10) Pituitary has the lowest level of MAO-B activity when compared with brain areas studied.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Function")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Noradrenaline_breakdown.svg/500px-Noradrenaline_breakdown.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Noradrenaline_breakdown.svg)

Norepinephrine degradation. Monoamine oxidase is shown left in the blue box.[[11]](#cite_note-Rang&Dale6th-11-4-11)

Monoamine oxidases catalyze the [oxidative deamination](/wiki/Oxidative_deamination "Oxidative deamination") of monoamines. In the first part of the reaction, [cofactor](/wiki/Cofactor_(biochemistry) "Cofactor (biochemistry)") [FAD](/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide "Flavin adenine dinucleotide") oxidizes the substrate yielding the corresponding [imine](/wiki/Imine "Imine") which converts the cofactor into its reduced form [FADH2](/wiki/FADH2 "FADH2"). The imine is then non-enzymatically hydrolyzed to the corresponding [ketone](/wiki/Ketone "Ketone") (or [aldehyde](/wiki/Aldehyde "Aldehyde")) and [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"). [Oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") is used to restore the reduced [FADH2](/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide "Flavin adenine dinucleotide") cofactor back to the active [FAD](/wiki/FAD "FAD") form. Monoamine [oxidases](/wiki/Oxidase "Oxidase") contain the covalently bound [cofactor](/wiki/Cofactor_(biochemistry) "Cofactor (biochemistry)") [FAD](/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide "Flavin adenine dinucleotide") and are, thus, classified as [flavoproteins](/wiki/Flavoprotein "Flavoprotein"). Monoamine oxidase A and B share roughly 70% of their structure and both have substrate binding sites that are predominantly [hydrophobic](/wiki/Hydrophobic "Hydrophobic"). Two [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine") residues (398, 435 within [MAO-B](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_B "Monoamine oxidase B"), 407 and 444 within [MAO-A](/wiki/MAO-A "MAO-A")) in the binding pocket that are commonly involved in inhibitor activity have been hypothesized to be relevant to orienting substrates, and mutations of these residues are relevant to mental health. Four main models have been proposed for the mechanism of [electron transfer](/wiki/Electron_transfer "Electron transfer") (single electron transfer, hydrogen atom transfer, nucleophilic model, and hydride transfer[[12]](#cite_note-12)) although there is insufficient evidence to support any of them.[[13]](#cite_note-pmid22022344-13)

In 2021, it was discovered that MAO-B does not mediate dopamine [catabolism](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism") in the rodent [striatum](/wiki/Striatum "Striatum") but instead participates in striatal [γ-aminobutyric acid](/wiki/%CE%93-aminobutyric_acid "Γ-aminobutyric acid") (GABA) synthesis from [putrescine](/wiki/Putrescine "Putrescine") and that synthesized GABA in turn inhibits [dopaminergic](/wiki/Dopaminergic "Dopaminergic") [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") in this brain area.[[14]](#cite_note-NamSaJu2022-14)[[15]](#cite_note-ChoKimSim2021-15) It has been found that MAO-B, via the putrescine pathway, importantly mediates GABA synthesis in [astrocytes](/wiki/Astrocyte "Astrocyte") in various brain areas, including in the [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus"), [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum"), striatum, [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex"), and [substantia nigra pars compacta](/wiki/Substantia_nigra_pars_compacta "Substantia nigra pars compacta") (SNpc).[[14]](#cite_note-NamSaJu2022-14)[[15]](#cite_note-ChoKimSim2021-15) These findings may warrant a rethinking of the actions of [MAO-B inhibitors](/wiki/MAO-B_inhibitor "MAO-B inhibitor") in the treatment of [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease").[[14]](#cite_note-NamSaJu2022-14)[[15]](#cite_note-ChoKimSim2021-15)

## Substrates and specificities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Substrates and specificities")]

Monoamine oxidases are well known [enzymes](/wiki/Enzymes "Enzymes") in [pharmacology](/wiki/Pharmacology "Pharmacology"), since they are the target for the action of a number of [monoamine oxidase inhibitor](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor "Monoamine oxidase inhibitor") [drugs](/wiki/Drugs "Drugs"). MAO-A is particularly important in the [catabolism](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism") of monoamines ingested in food. Both MAOs are also vital to the inactivation of [monoamine neurotransmitters](/wiki/Monoamine_neurotransmitter "Monoamine neurotransmitter"), for which they display different [specificities](/wiki/Enzyme#Specificity "Enzyme").[*[medical citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)")*]

* [Serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), and [epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine") are mainly broken down by MAO-A.[*[medical citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)")*]
* [Phenethylamine](/wiki/Phenethylamine "Phenethylamine") and [benzylamine](/wiki/Benzylamine "Benzylamine") are mainly broken down by MAO-B.[*[medical citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)")*]
* Both forms metabolize [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"), [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine"), and [tryptamine](/wiki/Tryptamine "Tryptamine");[[16]](#cite_note-pmid11559028-16) however, some evidence suggests MAO-B may not be responsible for a significant amount of dopamine degradation.[[17]](#cite_note-pmid34244591-17)

Specific reactions catalyzed by MAO include:[[18]](#cite_note-Tipton2018-18)[[19]](#cite_note-BortolatoShih2011-19)

* [Serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") to [5-hydroxyindoleacetaldehyde](/wiki/5-Hydroxyindoleacetaldehyde "5-Hydroxyindoleacetaldehyde") (5-HIAL; 5-HIAAL; serotonin aldehyde)[[20]](#cite_note-BortolatoChenShih2010-20)[[21]](#cite_note-MatthesMosienkoBashammakh2010-21)
* [5-Methoxytryptamine](/wiki/5-Methoxytryptamine "5-Methoxytryptamine") ([melatonin](/wiki/Melatonin "Melatonin") metabolite) to [5-methoxyindoleacetaldehyde](/w/index.php?title=5-methoxyindoleacetaldehyde&action=edit&redlink=1 "5-methoxyindoleacetaldehyde (page does not exist)") (5-MIAL; 5-MIAAL)[[22]](#cite_note-SlominskiTobinZmijewski2008-22)
* [Tryptamine](/wiki/Tryptamine "Tryptamine") to [indoleacetaldehyde](/wiki/Indole-3-acetaldehyde "Indole-3-acetaldehyde") (IAAL; tryptamine aldehyde)[[19]](#cite_note-BortolatoShih2011-19)
* [Dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") to [3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetaldehyde](/wiki/3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetaldehyde "3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetaldehyde") (DOPAL; dopamine aldehyde)[[23]](#cite_note-MeiserWeindlHiller2013-23)
* [Norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine") to [3,4-dihydroxymandelaldehyde](/wiki/3,4-dihydroxymandelaldehyde "3,4-dihydroxymandelaldehyde") (DHMAL; norepinephrine/epinephrine aldehyde)[[24]](#cite_note-KawamuraEisenhoferKopin2002-24)
* [Epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine") to [3,4-dihydroxymandelaldehyde](/wiki/3,4-dihydroxymandelaldehyde "3,4-dihydroxymandelaldehyde") (DHMAL; norepinephrine/epinephrine aldehyde)[[24]](#cite_note-KawamuraEisenhoferKopin2002-24)
* [Normetanephrine](/wiki/Normetanephrine "Normetanephrine") to [3-methoxy-4-hydroxymandelaldehyde](/w/index.php?title=3-methoxy-4-hydroxymandelaldehyde&action=edit&redlink=1 "3-methoxy-4-hydroxymandelaldehyde (page does not exist)") (MHMAL; normetanephrine/metanephrine aldehyde)[[24]](#cite_note-KawamuraEisenhoferKopin2002-24)
* [Metanephrine](/wiki/Metanephrine "Metanephrine") to [3-methoxy-4-hydroxymandelaldehyde](/w/index.php?title=3-methoxy-4-hydroxymandelaldehyde&action=edit&redlink=1 "3-methoxy-4-hydroxymandelaldehyde (page does not exist)") (MHMAL; normetanephrine/metanephrine aldehyde)[[24]](#cite_note-KawamuraEisenhoferKopin2002-24)
* [3-Methoxytyramine](/wiki/3-Methoxytyramine "3-Methoxytyramine") to [3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde](/w/index.php?title=3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde&action=edit&redlink=1 "3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde (page does not exist)") (HMPAL)[[25]](#cite_note-BandalaCárdenas-RodríguezMendoza-Torreblanca2023-25)
* [Phenethylamine](/wiki/Phenethylamine "Phenethylamine") to [phenylacetaldehyde](/wiki/Phenylacetaldehyde "Phenylacetaldehyde") (PAAL)[[26]](#cite_note-DalvieDi2019-26)
* [Tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine") to [4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde](/wiki/4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde "4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde") (HPAL)[[19]](#cite_note-BortolatoShih2011-19)
* [Benzylamine](/wiki/Benzylamine "Benzylamine") to [benzaldehyde](/wiki/Benzaldehyde "Benzaldehyde")[[27]](#cite_note-Holt2018-27)

Other endogenous substrates of MAO include [telemethylhistamine](/w/index.php?title=Telemethylhistamine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Telemethylhistamine (page does not exist)"), a metabolite of [histamine](/wiki/Histamine "Histamine"), and [*N*-acetylputrescine](/wiki/N-acetylputrescine "N-acetylputrescine"), a metabolite of [putrescine](/wiki/Putrescine "Putrescine") and a [precursor](/wiki/Precursor_(biochemistry) "Precursor (biochemistry)") and [metabolic intermediate](/wiki/Metabolic_intermediate "Metabolic intermediate") in a minor [metabolic pathway](/wiki/Metabolic_pathway "Metabolic pathway") resulting in the synthesis of [γ-aminobutyric acid](/wiki/%CE%93-aminobutyric_acid "Γ-aminobutyric acid") (GABA).[[28]](#cite_note-BenedettiDostert1994-28)[[29]](#cite_note-AmbroziakMaśliński1988-29)[[30]](#cite_note-WatanabeMaemuraKanbara2002-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Seiler2004-31)[[15]](#cite_note-ChoKimSim2021-15)

Besides [endogenous](/wiki/Endogenous "Endogenous") compounds, a variety of [exogenous](/wiki/Exogenous "Exogenous") compounds and [drugs](/wiki/Drug "Drug") are substrates of the MAOs.[[28]](#cite_note-BenedettiDostert1994-28)[[32]](#cite_note-PangTangGuo2022-32)[[33]](#cite_note-StrolinBenedettiDostertTipton1988-33)[[34]](#cite_note-Zetin2013-34) Examples include [substituted phenethylamine](/wiki/Substituted_phenethylamine "Substituted phenethylamine") [sympathomimetics](/wiki/Sympathomimetic "Sympathomimetic") and [sympatholytics](/wiki/Sympatholytic "Sympatholytic") like [phenylephrine](/wiki/Phenylephrine "Phenylephrine"), [propranolol](/wiki/Propranolol "Propranolol"), and [pronethalol](/wiki/Pronethalol "Pronethalol"), [substituted tryptamine](/wiki/Substituted_tryptamine "Substituted tryptamine") [serotonergic agents](/wiki/Serotonergic_drug "Serotonergic drug") like [dimethyltryptamine](/wiki/Dimethyltryptamine "Dimethyltryptamine") (DMT), [5-MeO-DMT](/wiki/5-MeO-DMT "5-MeO-DMT"), [bufotenin](/wiki/Bufotenin "Bufotenin"), [almotriptan](/wiki/Almotriptan "Almotriptan"), [rizatriptan](/wiki/Rizatriptan "Rizatriptan"), and [sumatriptan](/wiki/Sumatriptan "Sumatriptan"), and other compounds like [bicifadine](/wiki/Bicifadine "Bicifadine"), [citalopram](/wiki/Citalopram "Citalopram"), [CP-409092](/w/index.php?title=CP-409092&action=edit&redlink=1 "CP-409092 (page does not exist)"), [KW-2449](/w/index.php?title=KW-2449&action=edit&redlink=1 "KW-2449 (page does not exist)"), [milacemide](/wiki/Milacemide "Milacemide"), [MPTP](/wiki/MPTP "MPTP"), [nomifensine](/wiki/Nomifensine "Nomifensine"), [primaquine](/wiki/Primaquine "Primaquine"), [rivaroxaban](/wiki/Rivaroxaban "Rivaroxaban"), [sertraline](/wiki/Sertraline "Sertraline"), and [ticlopidine](/wiki/Ticlopidine "Ticlopidine"), among others.[[28]](#cite_note-BenedettiDostert1994-28)[[32]](#cite_note-PangTangGuo2022-32)[[33]](#cite_note-StrolinBenedettiDostertTipton1988-33)[[34]](#cite_note-Zetin2013-34) [Haloperidol](/wiki/Haloperidol "Haloperidol") is another possible substrate of MAO, which may contribute to formation of its [neurotoxic](/wiki/Monoaminergic_neurotoxin "Monoaminergic neurotoxin") metabolite [HPP+](/wiki/HPP%2B "HPP+").[[28]](#cite_note-BenedettiDostert1994-28)

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

Because of the vital role that MAOs play in the inactivation of [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter"), MAO dysfunction (too much or too little MAO activity) is thought to contribute to a number of psychiatric and neurological disorders. Unusually high or low levels of MAOs in the body have been associated with [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia"),[[35]](#cite_note-pmid943955-35)[[36]](#cite_note-pmid1267046-36) [depression](/wiki/Clinical_depression "Clinical depression"),[[37]](#cite_note-pmid17088501-37) [attention deficit disorder](/wiki/Attention_deficit_disorder "Attention deficit disorder"),[[38]](#cite_note-38) [substance abuse](/wiki/Substance_abuse "Substance abuse"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) migraines,[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) and irregular sexual maturation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Monoamine oxidase inhibitors](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor "Monoamine oxidase inhibitor") are one of the major classes of drug prescribed for the treatment of depression, although they are often last-line treatment due to risk of the drug's interaction with diet or other drugs. Excessive levels of [catecholamines](/wiki/Catecholamine "Catecholamine") ([epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine"), [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), and [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine")) may lead to a [hypertensive crisis](/wiki/Hypertensive_emergency "Hypertensive emergency"), and excessive levels of [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") may lead to [serotonin syndrome](/wiki/Serotonin_syndrome "Serotonin syndrome").[*[medical citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)")*]

In fact, MAO-A inhibitors act as antidepressant and anti-anxiety agents, whereas MAO-B inhibitors are used alone or in combination to treat [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease") and [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease").[[42]](#cite_note-pmid15279566-42) Some research suggests that certain phenotypes of depression, such as those with anxiety, and "atypical" symptoms involving psychomotor retardation, weight gain and interpersonal sensitivity respond better to MAO inhibitors than other classes of anti-depressant. However the findings related to this have not been consistent.[[43]](#cite_note-pmid32931110-43) MAOIs may be effective in treatment resistant depression, especially when it does not respond to tricyclic antidepressants.[[44]](#cite_note-pmid15552546-44)

### Parasite interactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Parasite interactions")]

[Sleeping sickness](/wiki/African_trypanosomiasis "African trypanosomiasis") - caused by [trypanosomes](/wiki/Trypanosoma "Trypanosoma") - gets its name from the sleep disruption it causes in mammals. That sleep disruption is caused, at least in part, by trypanosomes' tendency to disrupt MAO activity in the [orexin](/wiki/Orexin "Orexin") system.[[45]](#cite_note-Kristensson-et-al-2010-45)

### Animal models

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Animal models")]

There are significant differences in MAO activity in different species. Dopamine is primarily deaminated by [MAO-A](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_A "Monoamine oxidase A") in rats, but by [MAO-B](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_B "Monoamine oxidase B") in [vervet monkeys](/wiki/Vervet_monkey "Vervet monkey") and humans.[[46]](#cite_note-Garrick_Murphy-46)

Mice unable to produce either MAO-A or MAO-B display [autistic-like](/wiki/Autism_spectrum "Autism spectrum") traits.[[47]](#cite_note-pmid22850464-47) These [knockout mice](/wiki/Knockout_mouse "Knockout mouse") display an increased response to stress.[[48]](#cite_note-pmid14697877-48)

#### Arthropods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Arthropods")]

##### Insects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Insects")]

Insect brains express MAOs,[[49]](#cite_note-Gripois-et-al-1977-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Evans-1980-50)[[51]](#cite_note-Lund-et-al-1979-51) and some [insecticides](/wiki/Insecticide "Insecticide")[[52]](#cite_note-Aziz-Knowles-1973-52)[[51]](#cite_note-Lund-et-al-1979-51) work by inhibiting them. An MAOI effect is especially important for [chlordimeform](/wiki/Chlordimeform "Chlordimeform")[[52]](#cite_note-Aziz-Knowles-1973-52)[[51]](#cite_note-Lund-et-al-1979-51)[[53]](#cite_note-Beeman-Matsumura-1974-53) (although one result shows little or no effect in *[Periplaneta americana](/wiki/American_cockroach "American cockroach")*);[[54]](#cite_note-Sloley-et-al-1985-54) and [dieldrin](/wiki/Dieldrin "Dieldrin") may[[49]](#cite_note-Gripois-et-al-1977-49) or may not[[50]](#cite_note-Evans-1980-50) be an MAOI in *[Locusta migratoria](/wiki/Migratory_locust "Migratory locust")*.[*[medical citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)")*]

##### Acari

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Acari")]

MAO activity has been detected in *[Rhipicephalus microplus](/wiki/Rhipicephalus_microplus "Rhipicephalus microplus")* and chlordimeform is an MAOI in *R. m.*.[[55]](#cite_note-Atkinson-et-al-1974-55)

## Genetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Genetics")]

The [genes](/wiki/Gene "Gene") encoding MAO-A and MAO-B are located side-by-side on the short arm of the [X chromosome](/wiki/X_chromosome "X chromosome"), and have about 70% sequence similarity. Rare mutations in the gene are associated with [Brunner syndrome](/wiki/Brunner_syndrome "Brunner syndrome").[*[medical citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)")*]

A study based on the [Dunedin cohort](/wiki/Dunedin_cohort "Dunedin cohort") concluded that maltreated children with a low-activity polymorphism in the [promoter](/wiki/Promoter_(biology) "Promoter (biology)") region of the MAO-A gene were more likely to develop [antisocial conduct disorders](/wiki/Conduct_disorder "Conduct disorder") than maltreated children with the high-activity variant.[[56]](#cite_note-pmid12161658-56) Out of the 442 total males in the study (maltreated or not), 37% had the low activity variant. Of the 13 maltreated males with low MAO-A activity, 11 had been assessed as exhibiting [adolescent conduct disorder](/wiki/Conduct_disorder "Conduct disorder") and 4 were convicted for violent offenses. The suggested mechanism for this effect is the decreased ability of those with low MAO-A activity to quickly degrade norepinephrine, the synaptic neurotransmitter involved in [sympathetic](/wiki/Sympathetic_nervous_system "Sympathetic nervous system") arousal and rage. This is argued to provide direct support for the idea that genetic susceptibility to disease is not determined at birth, but varies with exposure to environmental influences. However, most individuals with conduct disorder or convictions did not have low activity of MAO-A; maltreatment was found to have caused stronger predisposition for antisocial behavior than differences in MAO-A activity.[*[medical citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)")*]

The claim that an interaction between low MAO-A activity and maltreatment would cause anti-social behavior has been criticized since the predisposition towards anti-social behavior could equally well have been caused by *other* genes inherited from abusive parents.[[57]](#cite_note-isbn0-521-82818-X-57)

A possible link between predisposition to [novelty seeking](/wiki/Neophilia "Neophilia") and a [genotype](/wiki/Genotype "Genotype") of the MAO-A gene has been found.[[58]](#cite_note-pmid16538181-58)

A particular variant (or [genotype](/wiki/Genotype "Genotype")), dubbed "[warrior gene](/wiki/Warrior_gene "Warrior gene")" in the popular press, was over-represented in [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"). This supported earlier studies finding different proportions of variants in different ethnic groups. This is the case for many genetic variants, with 33% White/Non-Hispanic, 61% Asian/Pacific Islanders having the low-activity MAO-A [promoter](/wiki/Promoter_(biology) "Promoter (biology)") variant.[[59]](#cite_note-pmid9799080-59)

## Aging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Aging")]

Unlike many other enzymes, MAO-B activity is increased during aging in the brain of humans and other mammals.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Increased MAO-B activity was also found in the [pineal gland](/wiki/Pineal_gland "Pineal gland") of aging rats.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10) This may contribute to lowered levels of monoamines in aged brain and pineal gland.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)[[61]](#cite_note-61)
